title: Super not an answer to all your tax issues
full_text:
Superannuation has been a big winner from this month’s budget changes that introduced higher taxes for investors using trusts, negative gearing and the capital gains tax discount.
Super became one of the few options with a minimum tax rate below 30 per cent. Investment earnings pay just 15 per cent tax within super, and effectively just 10 per cent tax is paid on capital gains most of the time. And once you retire after 60 and switch super to a pension-paying phase, the tax rate is generally zero.
However, while super’s status has just been turbocharged by the government’s fresh assault on most other investment taxes, it is not the answer for everyone.
There are a few key reasons why.
Locked up Superannuation’s strict preservation rules mean that it generally cannot be withdrawn until after age 60 and the fund member retires. For some people, that can mean 40-plus years.
While pumping more cash into super now may make sense for people in their 50s and 60s, if you’re in your 20s or 30s you still have decades before you are allowed to touch it.
Young adults typically have more financial pressures around housing, education and future family needs, so super is likely to sit on the backburner – even if it now makes much better sense from a tax-saving perspective.
Asset limits Older and wealthier Australians can use super to a greater advantage because they generally have more money and fewer years before retirement, but it is no longer an unlimited parking spot for piles of wealth.
The federal government’s new taxes on high super balances begin on July 1, resulting in people with more than $3m in super paying 30 per cent tax, and those with $10m-plus paying 40 per cent.
However, in reality the average superannuation balance for those aged 60-64 is not much more than $355,000, so the vast majority of people have plenty of wiggle room left when it comes to being in danger of breaching those limits.
Rule changes This is a big worry for many super fund investors, perhaps too scared to lock away more of their money in superannuation only to see future governments change the goalposts again.
Constant rule changes by both sides of federal parliament have been a feature of super since it became compulsory in the early 1990s. But after all the tweaking and tinkering, it remains the lowest-taxed investment structure for building and preserving wealth in Australia.
Since the budget changes were announced on May 12, funds have not experienced a huge surge of money going into super, apart from the seasonal rise that often occurs at this time of year as people pump in cash before June 30.
Perhaps savers are worried about super being next on Jim Chalmers’ hit list, but super has been championed by Labor for decades so there is hope it will be left alone in the coming years.
Superannuation funds and super industry groups would be hoping this too, and say stability is vital to give savers confidence.
Association of Superannuation Funds of Australia CEO Mary Delahunty said this week that super was “the best deal there is for everyday Australians looking to invest”, and she noted that people expected its concessional tax treatment to remain stable.
“The industry is confident that the government understands why that stability is so crucial,” she said.
AustralianSuper deputy chief executive Rose Kerlin said it was still too early to say whether the budget announcements had caused a direct rise in contributions, and she noted that super today offered more investment choice and control than many people realised.
“Australians can invest in shares, ETFs, term deposits and more through their super to build long-term retirement wealth, in a tax-friendly environment,” Kerlin said.
UniSuper’s state manager advice for Victoria and Tasmania, Derek Gascoigne, said risks around legislative change came up in most conversations about long-term investing.
“As this budget has demonstrated, it doesn’t only apply to super,” he said. Gascoigne said the budget tax changes reinforced the attractiveness of super. “The system has a legislated objective to preserve savings and deliver income for a dignified retirement, which thereby provides a degree of structural stability,” he said.
